questions.question:
The number of additions and multiplications involved in performing Gaussian elimination on any m x n upper triangular matrix is of the order

A. O(n)
B. O(n^2)
C. O(n^3)
D. O(n^4)
questions.to_find: Computational complexity of Gaussian elimination on an upper triangular matrix

understand.plain: Gaussian elimination converts a matrix to row echelon form by eliminating entries below each pivot. When the matrix is already upper triangular, no row operations are needed below the diagonal. The only work remaining is the back-substitution phase, which requires visiting each of the n rows and performing operations proportional to the row index, leading to O(n^2) total operations.

keywords.explain: A square matrix where all entries below the main diagonal are zero. No forward elimination is needed.
Algorithm to reduce a matrix to row echelon form by subtracting multiples of one row from another.
keywords.example: Eliminates variables one at a time from the bottom of each column.

keywords.explain: Process of solving for unknowns starting from the last equation upward after forward elimination.
keywords.example: If the matrix is already upper triangular, only back substitution is needed: O(1)+O(2)+...+O(n) = O(n^2).

understand.visual_alt: An upper triangular n x n matrix requires only back substitution whose total work sums to O(n^2).

given.aim: Find the order of arithmetic operations for Gaussian elimination on an upper triangular m x n matrix

terms.meaning: All entries a_ij = 0 for i > j; no forward elimination step is needed
terms.connects: Since it is already in row echelon form, only back substitution is performed

terms.meaning: For a general n x n matrix: O(n^3); for upper triangular: 0 extra operations
terms.example: No entries below diagonal need zeroing out
terms.connects: Reduces the problem to counting back-substitution operations

terms.meaning: Row k requires k-1 multiplications and k-1 subtractions; summed over n rows gives O(n^2)
terms.connects: Determines the overall complexity for an already upper triangular matrix

given.plan: Count only back-substitution operations since forward elimination is not needed for an upper triangular matrix. The total is proportional to sum_{k=1}^{n}(k) = n(n+1)/2 = O(n^2).

steps.title: Identify that forward elimination is skipped
steps.formula_raw: Upper triangular => a_ij = 0 for all i > j
steps.apply: No row operations are needed to zero out entries below the diagonal. The matrix is already in row echelon form.
steps.note: This eliminates the dominant O(n^3) cost of general Gaussian elimination.

steps.title: Count back-substitution operations
steps.apply: Row n: 0 operations; row n-1: 1 operation; ...; row 1: n-1 operations. Total = 0+1+2+...+(n-1) = n(n-1)/2.
steps.note: This is exactly O(n^2), matching option B.

solution.result: The complexity is O(n^2), so the answer is B.

questions.formula_note: Back substitution on an n x n upper triangular system costs sum_{k=0}^{n-1} k = n(n-1)/2 arithmetic operations, which is O(n^2).

questions.question:
Consider a system of linear equations PX = Q where P: R^{k-1} -> R^{k-1} and Q: R^{k-1}. Suppose P has an LU decomposition, P = LU, where

L = [[1,0,0],[l_21,1,0],[l_31,l_32,1]]  and  U = [[u_11,u_12,u_13],[0,u_22,u_23],[0,0,u_33]]

Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE?

A. The system PX = Q can be solved by first solving LY = Q and then UX = Y.
B. If P is invertible, then both L and U are invertible.
C. If P is singular, then at least one of the diagonal elements of U is zero.
D. If P is symmetric, then both L and U are symmetric.
questions.to_find: True statements about LU decomposition of matrix P

understand.plain: LU decomposition factors a square matrix P into a lower triangular matrix L (with 1s on diagonal) and an upper triangular matrix U. Solving PX = Q becomes two triangular solves: LY = Q followed by UX = Y. Key properties link the invertibility and singularity of P to those of L and U, since det(P) = det(L)*det(U) = det(U) (because det(L) = 1).

keywords.explain: Factoring P = LU where L is unit lower triangular and U is upper triangular. Allows efficient solution of linear systems.
Lower triangular matrix with all diagonal entries equal to 1. Always invertible; det = 1.
A matrix with determinant zero. For P = LU, det(P) = det(U), so P singular implies det(U) = 0, meaning at least one diagonal of U is zero.
keywords.example: If u_33 = 0 in an upper triangular U, then det(U) = 0.

understand.visual_alt: LU decomposition splits system PX=Q into forward solve LY=Q and back solve UX=Y.

given.aim: Determine which statements about P = LU are true given P is a square matrix with unit lower triangular L and upper triangular U

terms.meaning: L is unit lower triangular (diagonal = 1), U is upper triangular
terms.connects: Invertibility of P depends entirely on invertibility of U

terms.term: Two-step solve: LY = Q, then UX = Y
terms.meaning: Since P = LU, PX = LUX = L(UX) = LY = Q where Y = UX
terms.example: Forward substitution gives Y; back substitution gives X
terms.connects: Statement A: this is the standard way to use LU factorization

terms.meaning: det(U) = product of diagonal entries u_11*u_22*u_33; if this is zero, at least one diagonal is zero
terms.example: If P singular then det(U) = 0 => some u_ii = 0
terms.connects: Statement C: direct consequence of the determinant formula for triangular matrices

given.plan: Evaluate each statement using properties det(P) = det(U), the two-step solve structure, and symmetry of L and U. A: True (standard LU solve). B: True (P invertible <=> det(P) != 0 <=> det(U) != 0 <=> U invertible; L always invertible). C: True (P singular => det(U) = 0 => some u_ii = 0). D: False (L has 1s on diagonal and non-symmetric off-diagonal in general; U is upper not lower).

steps.title: Verify Statement A: two-step solve
steps.formula_raw: PX = LUX = Q => set Y = UX, solve LY = Q then UX = Y
steps.apply: Since P = LU, we can write L(UX) = Q. Define Y = UX. Then LY = Q can be solved by forward substitution giving Y, then UX = Y by back substitution gives X.

steps.title: Verify Statement B: invertibility
steps.apply: P invertible <=> det(P) != 0 <=> det(U) != 0 <=> U invertible. L is unit lower triangular so det(L) = 1, making L always invertible.

steps.title: Verify Statement C: singular P implies zero diagonal in U
steps.apply: P singular => det(P) = 0 => det(U) = 0 => product of diagonals of U is zero => at least one u_ii = 0.

steps.title: Verify Statement D: symmetry
steps.formula_raw: P = LU is symmetric only if L = U^T under special conditions
steps.apply: L is lower triangular with 1s on diagonal; U is upper triangular. For both to be symmetric, L would need to equal U^T which is generally not the case. A symmetric P does not imply L or U are individually symmetric.

solution.result: Statements A, B, and C are TRUE.

questions.formula_note: For P = LU with unit lower triangular L, det(P) = det(U), so all invertibility and singularity properties of P map directly onto U.

questions.question:
A square matrix is singular whenever

A. The rows are linearly independent
B. The columns are linearly independent
C. The rows are linearly dependent
D. None of the above
questions.answer: C. The rows are linearly dependent
questions.to_find: Condition that makes a square matrix singular

understand.plain: A square matrix is singular if and only if its determinant is zero, which is equivalent to saying that its rows (or equivalently its columns) are linearly dependent. Linear dependence means at least one row can be expressed as a linear combination of the others, making the rank less than n and the determinant zero.

keywords.explain: A square matrix with determinant equal to zero. It is not invertible and its rows/columns are linearly dependent.
keywords.example: [[1,2],[2,4]] is singular because row 2 = 2 * row 1, det = 0.

keywords.explain: A set of vectors is linearly dependent if one can be written as a linear combination of the others, i.e., there exist scalars c_1,...,c_n not all zero such that c_1*v_1 + ... + c_n*v_n = 0.
The number of linearly independent rows (or columns) in a matrix. A matrix is singular iff rank < n.
keywords.example: An n x n matrix with rank n-1 or less is singular.

understand.visual_alt: A square matrix is singular when rows are linearly dependent, collapsing the row space to a subspace of dimension less than n.

given.aim: Identify which condition among the options guarantees that a square matrix is singular

terms.meaning: No row is a linear combination of others; implies full rank and non-singular
terms.example: [[1,0],[0,1]] has independent rows and is non-singular
terms.connects: Options A and B describe non-singular conditions

terms.meaning: At least one row is a linear combination of others; rank < n; det = 0
terms.connects: Option C correctly identifies the singular condition

terms.meaning: det(A) = 0 if and only if rows are linearly dependent (for square matrices)
terms.example: Row operations that make a zero row show determinant is zero
terms.connects: Bridges the definition of singular with linear dependence

given.plan: Recall that det(A) = 0 <=> rows (or columns) are linearly dependent <=> matrix is singular. Check each option against this equivalence.

steps.title: Recall the definition of a singular matrix
steps.formula_raw: A is singular <=> det(A) = 0 <=> rank(A) < n
steps.apply: A square n x n matrix is singular if and only if its determinant is zero, equivalently when its rank is less than n.
steps.note: This is the standard definition used to evaluate each option.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A: Linearly independent rows => full rank => non-singular. B: Linearly independent columns => same conclusion. C: Linearly dependent rows => rank < n => det = 0 => singular. D: Not applicable since C is correct.

solution.result: The answer is C: the rows are linearly dependent.

questions.formula_note: A square matrix is singular if and only if its rows (or columns) are linearly dependent, which is equivalent to det(A) = 0.

questions.question:
Assume that the matrix A given below has factorization of the form LU = PA, where L is lower-triangular with all diagonal elements equal to 1, U is upper-triangular, and P is a permutation matrix.

A = [[2, 5, 9], [4, 1, 5], [8, 2, 3]]

Compute L, U, and P using Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting.
questions.answer: L = [[1,0,0],[1/4,1,0],[1/2,9/14,1]], U = [[8,2,3],[0,9/2,33/4],[0,0,53/8]], P = row-permutation swapping row 1 and row 3 then row 2 and row 3 (permutation matrix placing original rows in order 3,1,2)
questions.to_find: L, U, and P from Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting of matrix A

understand.plain: LU decomposition with partial pivoting factors a matrix A as PA = LU where P is a permutation matrix that reorders rows to place the largest pivot first. This improves numerical stability. We perform Gaussian elimination column by column, always swapping to bring the largest element in the column to the pivot position, then recording the multipliers in L.

keywords.explain: At each elimination step, swap rows so the element with the largest absolute value in the current column (at or below the diagonal) becomes the pivot. Improves numerical stability.
keywords.example: In column 1 of A, entries are 2, 4, 8. Swap row 1 and row 3 to use 8 as pivot.

keywords.explain: A matrix with exactly one 1 in each row and column, encoding row swaps applied during pivoting. PA = LU.
The factor m_ij = a_ij / a_jj used to eliminate entry (i,j). These multipliers fill the lower triangular part of L.
keywords.example: If pivot is 8 and entry below is 4, multiplier = 4/8 = 1/2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 520 200" width="520" height="200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="0" y="0" width="520" height="200" rx="10" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="30" class="lbl ink">A = [[2,5,9],[4,1,5],[8,2,3]]</text><text x="20" y="60" class="sub ink">Step 1: Pivot col 1 => swap row1 & row3 (8 is max)</text><text x="20" y="85" class="sub ink">Step 2: Eliminate col 1 below pivot: m21=4/8=1/2, m31=2/8=1/4</text><text x="20" y="110" class="sub ink">Step 3: Pivot col 2 in submatrix => check remaining rows</text><text x="20" y="135" class="sub ink">Step 4: Record multipliers in L; upper part gives U</text><rect x="10" y="145" width="490" height="40" class="hi" rx="6"/><text x="20" y="170" class="tag ink">PA = LU  with  P = row-swap permutation, L = unit lower triangular, U = upper triangular</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Matrix A with entries shown undergoing row swaps and elimination steps to produce L, U, P factors.

given.aim: Find L (unit lower triangular), U (upper triangular), and P (permutation) such that PA = LU using Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting

terms.meaning: A = [[2,5,9],[4,1,5],[8,2,3]], the 3x3 matrix to be factored
terms.example: Row 3 has the largest first entry (8), so it becomes the first pivot row
terms.connects: Determines the sequence of row swaps in partial pivoting

terms.meaning: P reorders rows of A so that elimination proceeds with maximal pivots; L stores multipliers; U stores the reduced matrix
terms.example: If P swaps rows 1 and 3, then PA has 8 in position (1,1)
terms.connects: The algorithm output defines all three matrices simultaneously

terms.meaning: At each step k, find the row with the largest |a_ik| for i >= k and swap it with row k before computing multipliers
terms.example: Column 1: max is 8 at row 3; swap rows 1 and 3; multipliers are 4/8=1/2 and 2/8=1/4
terms.connects: Produces numerically stable L and U factors

given.plan: Apply Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting step by step. Track row swaps in P, record multipliers in L, and the resulting upper triangular form gives U.

steps.title: Initial matrix A and start partial pivoting on column 1
steps.apply: Column 1 entries: |2|=2, |4|=4, |8|=8. Max is 8 at row 3. Swap row 1 and row 3. Now: [[8,2,3],[4,1,5],[2,5,9]]. Record this swap in P: P swaps rows 1 and 3.

steps.title: Eliminate column 1 below pivot (row 1)
steps.note: Multipliers m21=1/2 and m31=1/4 go into L below diagonal in column 1.

steps.title: Partial pivot on column 2 of submatrix (rows 2 and 3)
steps.apply: Column 2 entries in submatrix: |0|=0 (row 2), |9/2|=4.5 (row 3). Max is 9/2 at row 3. Swap rows 2 and 3: [[8,2,3],[0,9/2,33/4],[0,0,7/2]]. Record this swap in P.
steps.note: After second swap, updated matrix is upper triangular form for U.

steps.title: Eliminate column 2 and read off U
steps.apply: After swapping rows 2 and 3 at step 3, row 3 has [0,0,7/2]. Entry (3,2) = 0, so m32 = 0/( 9/2) = 0. No elimination needed. U = [[8,2,3],[0,9/2,33/4],[0,0,7/2]].
steps.note: The matrix is now upper triangular. U is fully determined.

steps.title: Assemble L accounting for row swaps
steps.formula_raw: L = unit lower triangular with multipliers adjusted for swaps
steps.apply: L_11=1, L_22=1, L_33=1 (unit diagonal). After both swaps, the multipliers are placed correctly: m21 (from original step, after swap 2 this appears in row 3 position), yielding L = [[1,0,0],[1/4,1,0],[1/2,0,1]] before the second swap's effect. After tracking swaps properly: L = [[1,0,0],[1/2,1,0],[1/4,0,1]] adjusted to [[1,0,0],[1/4,1,0],[1/2,0,1]].
steps.note: The exact L entries depend on the order of swaps applied. Final: P encodes both swaps, L is unit lower triangular, U = [[8,2,3],[0,9/2,33/4],[0,0,7/2]].

steps.title: State the permutation matrix P
steps.formula_raw: P = product of elementary permutation matrices for each row swap
steps.apply: Swap 1: rows 1 and 3. Swap 2: rows 2 and 3 (in new ordering). Combined, P maps original row ordering (1,2,3) to (3,1,2), giving P = [[0,0,1],[1,0,0],[0,1,0]].

questions.formula_note: Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting produces PA = LU where P accumulates row swaps, multipliers m_ij = a_ij/a_jj fill L, and U is the resulting upper triangular matrix.

formulas.name: Back Substitution Cost for Upper Triangular System
LU Two-Step System Solve
formulas.plain: PX = LUX = Q => solve LY = Q (forward substitution), then UX = Y (back substitution)

formulas.name: LU Decomposition Determinant Relation
formulas.plain: det(P) = det(L) * det(U); for unit lower triangular L: det(L)=1, so det(P) = det(U)

formulas.name: Determinant of Triangular Matrix
formulas.plain: det(A) = product of diagonal entries = a_11 * a_22 * ... * a_nn (for triangular A)

formulas.name: Singular Matrix Equivalences
formulas.plain: A is singular <=> det(A) = 0 <=> rank(A) < n <=> rows (columns) are linearly dependent

formulas.name: Partial Pivoting Step
formulas.plain: At step k, select pivot row r = argmax_{i>=k} |a_ik|, swap rows k and r, then eliminate below

formulas.name: Gaussian Elimination Multiplier
formulas.plain: m_ik = a_ik / a_kk; row i <- row i - m_ik * row k; m_ik stored in L at position (i,k)
